concept.core_loop: Drag to swap adjacent gems for 3+ matches on the lower grid; an enemy line advances in real time down the battlefield above and chips your wall's HP continuously while in contact. RED matches blast the line back and damage it; BLUE matches repair the wall — and because these are the SAME swaps, every match is an offense-vs-defense choice (the shared resource is your attention to the board). FORCING FUNCTION: every ~6s the line telegraphs a heavy strike (a charging chevron with a shrinking countdown ring) that craters the wall UNLESS you land a blue match before it fires — which forces you to abandon whatever combo you were building and defend NOW; conversely, if you only ever defend, the line marches into the wall and overwhelms you, so you must also steal moments for red. So second to second the player is deciding: 'defend the telegraphed strike, push the line back, or risk one more swap to set up a bigger combo?' Chaining matches charges a NOVA that clears the whole line (the relief valve). Difficulty: advance speed, telegraph frequency, and reinforcement toughness all step up every ~20s up to a cap, so late game forces tighter offense/defense alternation and bigger combos.
mechanics: drag-to-swap match-3 on a 6-wide colored gem grid
match detection (3+ row/column) + gravity cascade + refill
real-time advancing enemy line that deals continuous chip damage to the wall on contact
shared-resource match effects: red matches damage and push back the line, blue matches repair wall HP — the same swaps serve both, creating offense/defense contention
TELEGRAPHED heavy strike every ~6s with a visible countdown ring; only a blue match landed in time prevents a large wall hit (the forcing function that reshapes match priority)
wall HP as the loss condition (game over + restart on tap when wall HP hits 0)
combo/chain meter that charges a screen-clearing NOVA (reward beat + relief valve)
score from survival time + enemies destroyed; HUD shows wall HP, nova charge, and the active telegraph countdown
difficulty ramp: advance speed + telegraph frequency + reinforcement toughness step up every ~20s up to a cap
concept.art_direction: Clean flat-neon on deep indigo (#0d0b1f). Gems are rounded-square primitives in five saturated hues with a soft additive glow and a small inner highlight so colors read instantly: red = attack, blue = shield, plus green, amber, violet as neutral fillers. The battlefield strip at the top sits on a darker band with a faint scrolling hex/grid pattern receding toward a horizon line for depth; the enemy line is a row of angular magenta chevrons creeping downward, and the wall is a thick cyan bar across the grid's top edge whose brightness and thickness track its HP. TELEGRAPH treatment: when the line charges a heavy strike, its lead chevron pulses red-hot, a shrinking countdown ring draws around it, and a danger vignette bleeds in from the top edge; landing a blue match in time fires a cyan shield-flare that visibly absorbs the blow. Other feedback beats: matched gems pop with a scale-up + hue-colored particle burst; a red match fires a bright tracer streak from the grid up to the line with a hit-flash; the nova meter pulses a ring as it charges and detonates a full-width white sweep; the whole screen flashes white and shakes when the wall is breached. A persistent HUD shows wall HP (top-left), nova charge (top-right), and the telegraph countdown in large high-contrast type.
concept.differentiation_notes: The blend itself is the hook: the grid is your only weapon AND your only shield, so every swap is an offense/defense decision, and a telegraphed strike repeatedly forces you to drop combo-building to defend. That fusion — not match-3 alone, not defense alone — is the game. Distinct from our own Prism Cascade (match-3 racing an abstract drain timer) and from tower defense (no grid-as-weapon).

issues: blend cohesion improved vs v1 but still partial: the forcing function reads as a periodic interrupt (puzzle <-> defend beat) rather than continuous fusion; owner: "definitely more of a blend than before, but still kinda not cohesive"
